text.title: CI Quality Gate Design Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that make CI gate design a one-shot decision per check rather than a recurring debate.

rule.statement: Every CI check MUST be classified into exactly one of three tiers: BLOCK (must pass for merge), WARN (runs and comments but does not gate), NIGHTLY (runs on schedule, alerts the team channel, never gates a PR). A check with no tier assignment is removed from CI until one is assigned.

rationale.source: DORA capability: deployment automation + change-fail rate balancing

rule.rationale: Without a tier model, teams default to "block everything" — which leads to working-around CI via skip flags — or "block nothing meaningful" — which lets regressions through. Three tiers cover the realistic decision space.

rule.statement: Every BLOCK-tier check MUST have a one-paragraph written rationale committed in ci-design.md: what regression this check prevents, what false-positive rate is acceptable, and the escalation path if the check is flapping. Verbal justification ("we've always run this") does not count.

rationale.source: Standard governance practice; without written rationale, deprecation arguments win against forgotten reasoning

rule.rationale: Two years later the team will debate removing the check. The written rationale is the only artifact that lets that decision be informed instead of reactive.

rule.statement: The total BLOCK-tier wall-clock budget on a PR MUST be defined in ci-design.md and enforced. Default targets: < 10 min for solo teams, < 15 min for small teams. New BLOCK additions that exceed budget MUST either move an existing BLOCK to WARN or be added at WARN themselves.
rule.rationale: PR cycle time above 30 min correlates strongly with batched PRs, larger diffs, and longer review queues. A budget keeps gate addition zero-sum, forcing real trade-offs.

rule.statement: A check is eligible for BLOCK tier only if it produces actionable feedback: the developer can read the failure and know what to do next. Checks that produce "exit code 1, see logs" or 200-line lint reports of pre-existing debt are demoted to WARN until their output is actionable.

rationale.source: Empirical CI failure-fatigue research; cf. Google's SET test-pyramid talks

rule.rationale: Non-actionable BLOCK checks teach developers to bypass them. Actionable WARN checks teach developers to fix things.

rule.statement: Each BLOCK check MUST document its escalation path: who is alerted on persistent failure (3+ days), what the workaround is, and the maintenance owner. A check whose maintenance owner is "the team" defaults to nobody and gets bypassed during incidents.
rule.rationale: Unowned checks are the largest source of "we just merged with --no-verify because no one knew how to fix it" incidents. Naming an owner is cheap; the absence is expensive.

example.title: Tier classification entry
example.code:
Check: unit-tests Tier: BLOCK Rationale: Prevents merging code that breaks core business logic. Average runtime 3min, failure rate 2%/PR (actionable: failed test name + assertion). Owner: backend-team. Escalation: 3 days flapping -> #ci channel.
Check: mutation-tests Tier: NIGHTLY Rationale: Mutation testing on full module is 25min. Too long for PR budget. Nightly run with alert on score regression. Owner: testing-developer. Escalation: 2 nightly fails -> issue.

example.title: Block-everything pattern
example.code: Check: lint, unit-tests, integration-tests, e2e, security-scan, mutation, accessibility, perf, docs-build, type-check, license-scan, dependency-audit Tier: BLOCK (all) Total runtime: 28min Rationale: "Quality matters"
example.note: No budget, no rationale per check, no actionable distinction. Developers will start to skip-merge.
